era_context: The High Middle Ages in Europe; the Crusader States are in a period of relative stability under Amalric I. Nur ad-Din Zengi controls Syria and coordinates with Egyptian forces. The Byzantine Empire under Manuel I maintains its diplomatic and military presence in the Mediterranean and Balkans. In East Asia, the Jin Dynasty controls northern China; the Southern Song Dynasty rules the south. Islamic scholarship and trade networks span the Mediterranean and Indian Ocean.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Pre-Columbian Americas (no Old World written records)
Central Asia and the Steppe (limited written records; oral traditions underrepresented)

events.title: Birth of Genghis Khan (Temüjin)
events.description: Temüjin, who would later be known as Genghis Khan, was born on the Mongolian steppe. His birth is traditionally dated to 1162, though some sources place it in 1155 or other years. Temüjin would eventually unify the Mongol tribes and establish the largest contiguous land empire in history. His early life was marked by hardship, tribal conflict, and the development of military and organizational skills that would define his later conquests.

sources.name: Rashid al-Din, Jami' al-tawarikh (Compendium of Chronicles)
Marco Polo, The Travels of Marco Polo

events.certainty_note: The birth date of Genghis Khan is uncertain. The Secret History of the Mongols, compiled after his death, gives 1162 as the traditional date, but modern scholars debate whether this is accurate or symbolic. Some sources place his birth in 1155 or other years. The date 1162 is conventional but not definitively confirmed.

events.title: Thomas Becket becomes Archbishop of Canterbury
events.description: Thomas Becket was appointed Archbishop of Canterbury by King Henry II. Becket, previously the king's chancellor and close advisor, was expected to serve the crown's interests in ecclesiastical matters. However, Becket's appointment marked a turning point in his life; he adopted a more austere lifestyle and increasingly championed the Church's independence from royal authority, leading to a dramatic conflict with Henry II that would culminate in his murder in 1170.

sources.name: William of Canterbury, Life of Thomas Becket
Edward Grim, Life of Thomas Becket
John of Salisbury, Life of Thomas Becket

events.certainty_note: Multiple contemporary and near-contemporary sources attest to Becket's appointment as Archbishop in 1162. The date is well established.

events.title: Fire at Canterbury Cathedral
events.description: A major fire destroyed much of Canterbury Cathedral, including the choir and parts of the nave. The fire necessitated a major reconstruction effort that would occupy the cathedral for years. The rebuilding was part of the broader Romanesque architectural flourishing in 12th-century England and reflected the cathedral's importance as the seat of the Archbishop of Canterbury.
events.certainty_note: Gervase of Canterbury provides a detailed contemporary account of the fire and its aftermath. The date of 1161 or 1162 is well established in his chronicle.

events.title: Nur ad-Din's consolidation of Syria
events.description: Nur ad-Din Zengi continued his consolidation of power in Syria and Mesopotamia, strengthening his control over key cities and coordinating with Egyptian forces against the Crusaders. His administration was marked by the promotion of Islamic scholarship, the construction of religious institutions, and the organization of jihad against the Crusader States. Nur ad-Din's reign represented a high point of Zengid power before his death in 1174.
key_figures: Nur ad-Din Mahmud ibn Zengi (Zengid ruler)
events.certainty_note: Nur ad-Din's consolidation of power occurred across the 1150s–1170s. The year 1162 is conventional; specific events of this year are not well documented in surviving sources.

disconfirming_evidence: The birth date of Genghis Khan (Temüjin) is highly uncertain. While 1162 is the traditional date given in the Secret History of the Mongols and later Islamic sources, modern scholars debate its accuracy. Some sources place his birth in 1155, 1167, or other years. The date 1162 may be symbolic or conventional rather than historically accurate. Additionally, the fire at Canterbury Cathedral is sometimes dated to 1161 rather than 1162; sources vary on the exact year.
historiographic_note: The year 1162 is moderately well documented in European sources, particularly regarding England and the Church. Islamic sources provide accounts of Syrian and Egyptian politics. However, most accounts were written decades or centuries after the events. The record is heavily weighted toward political, military, and ecclesiastical events; economic and social developments are underrepresented. For Central Asia and Mongolia, the historical record is extremely sparse; the Secret History of the Mongols, compiled after Genghis Khan's death, is the primary source for his early life, and its reliability for specific dates is disputed.

graph_edges.note: Temüjin's birth in 1162 (traditional date) marked the beginning of the life that would lead to his proclamation as Genghis Khan in 1206.
Becket's appointment as Archbishop in 1162 set in motion the conflict with Henry II that culminated in his murder in 1170.
The fire at Canterbury Cathedral in 1162 necessitated the reconstruction whose consecration occurred in 1164.
